description: Pipelined v3 — Carries over US-057 dark mode, patches all security/bug findings from the post-v2 review, polishes UX with skeletons/accessibility/mobile, and ships three expansive new features: custom stage pipelines, bulk CSV import, and a weekly email digest.

userStories.title: Dark mode with system preference detection and manual toggle
userStories.description: Carry-over from v2. As a user who prefers dark interfaces or works at night, I want the app to support dark mode that follows my system preference but can be overridden manually.
acceptanceCriteria: tailwind.config.js sets darkMode: 'class'

frontend/src/context/ThemeContext.jsx is created: reads system preference via window.matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)'), persists user override to localStorage under key 'pipelined_theme' ('light' | 'dark' | 'system'), and applies/removes the 'dark' class on document.documentElement
ThemeContext exports useTheme() hook that returns { theme, setTheme } where theme is the resolved value ('light' | 'dark') and setTheme cycles through modes
NavBar renders a sun/moon/monitor icon toggle button using the useTheme hook; icon updates to match current mode
dark: Tailwind variants are added to all major surfaces in NavBar, Dashboard, ApplicationList, DetailPanel, FilterBar, StatsBar, CalendarGrid, JobBoard, Login, Register, ManualAddForm
Vitest test in frontend/src/context/ThemeContext.test.jsx verifies: dark class is added/removed on documentElement, preference persists to localStorage, system preference is read on mount
npm test passes
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: ThemeContext.jsx and ThemeContext.test.jsx already exist as untracked files per git status — build on them rather than creating from scratch.

userStories.title: Fix calendar event security: user_id ownership scoping on list and delete
userStories.description: Critical security bug: a user can list another user's calendar events by guessing their application_id, and delete any event by ID without ownership verification. Both service functions must filter by user_id.
acceptanceCriteria: In cal/service.py list_events(): when application_id filter is provided, add a sub-query to verify the application belongs to the requesting user_id before listing its events (use $lookup or a preliminary applications.find_one({_id, user_id}))

In cal/service.py delete_event(): the deletion filter must include user_id — use the calendar_events collection filtered by both event_id and user_id; if no document is found, raise a CalendarEventNotFoundError (domain exception) that the router maps to 404
CalendarEventNotFoundError is defined in cal/service.py and mapped to HTTP 404 in cal/router.py
test_calendar.py adds two tests: (1) listing events with another user's application_id returns empty list, (2) deleting another user's event returns 404
All existing calendar tests still pass
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Security bug found in post-v2 review. calendar_events delete does not check user ownership. list_events with application_id filter does not verify the application belongs to the requesting user.

userStories.title: Fix text search cursor parsing — return 400 instead of crashing
userStories.description: When a user passes a malformed or tampered cursor in a text search query, the backend crashes with an unhandled ValueError or InvalidId exception. It should return a clean 400 error instead.
acceptanceCriteria: In applications/service.py, the cursor parsing block (where TEXT_SEARCH_CURSOR_SEP is split, float() and ObjectId() are called) is wrapped in a try/except (ValueError, InvalidId) block
On parse failure, raise an InvalidCursorError domain exception with message 'Invalid pagination cursor'
applications/router.py maps InvalidCursorError to HTTP 400 with code INVALID_CURSOR
InvalidCursorError is defined in applications/service.py
For standard (non-text-search) cursor parsing, apply the same guard: ObjectId() on the cursor string wrapped in try/except InvalidId → HTTP 400
test_applications.py adds a test: GET /applications?q=hello&cursor=not-valid-cursor returns 400 with code INVALID_CURSOR
All existing application tests still pass
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Post-v2 review finding: text search cursor uses float() and ObjectId() without error handling. A tampered cursor crashes the endpoint with a 500.

userStories.title: Validate analytics days parameter and filter checkbox accessibility fix
userStories.description: Two quick correctness fixes from the post-v2 review: (1) the analytics endpoint accepts any integer for days including negative/huge values; (2) FilterBar checkbox inputs lack proper id/htmlFor associations, breaking screen reader label click.
acceptanceCriteria: In applications/schemas.py, the analytics query schema validates days: int | None = Field(None, ge=1, le=365) using a Pydantic Field constraint; passing days=0 or days=999 returns 422
test_applications.py adds a test: GET /applications/analytics?days=-1 returns 422

In frontend/src/components/FilterBar.jsx, every checkbox input rendered inside CheckboxGroup gets a unique id (e.g. id={`filter-${groupKey}-${option}`}), and its wrapping label gets htmlFor pointing to that id
Verify keyboard navigation: clicking the label text toggles the checkbox (manual test in the browser is sufficient; document in progress.txt)
Vitest test for FilterBar verifies that input elements have id attributes and labels have matching htmlFor
All existing tests pass
Typecheck passes
npm test passes
userStories.notes: Two small but important fixes combined to save context overhead. Analytics days param allows absurd values; FilterBar checkboxes are inaccessible to keyboard/screen reader users.

userStories.title: ManualAddForm and bulk action loading states
userStories.description: As a user submitting the manual application form or triggering a bulk action, I see no feedback while the request is in-flight. The form can be double-submitted and bulk operations give no visual progress. Add pending states throughout.
acceptanceCriteria: ManualAddForm.jsx: while createApplication mutation isPending, the submit button is disabled and shows a spinner icon + 'Creating…' text instead of 'Add Application'
ManualAddForm.jsx: if the mutation errors, the error message from the API response (or a generic fallback) is shown inside the form above the submit button — not just a toast
ApplicationList.jsx bulk action bar: while bulk-delete or bulk-stage-update is pending, both action buttons are disabled and show a spinner
After a successful bulk operation, show a success toast: 'Deleted N applications' or 'Moved N applications to [stage]'
Vitest test for ManualAddForm: submit button is disabled during pending state
Vitest test for ApplicationList: bulk action buttons are disabled while mutation is pending
npm test passes
userStories.notes: Post-v2 review: ManualAddForm uses isPending from the hook but never renders it. Bulk action bar also lacks any in-flight feedback.

userStories.title: Centralized date formatting utility and consistent date display
userStories.description: As a developer maintaining the frontend, dates are formatted three different ways across components (toISODate, toLocaleDateString with no locale, toLocaleDateString with en-US options). This causes inconsistency and timezone bugs. Centralise all date formatting.
acceptanceCriteria: Create frontend/src/lib/dateUtils.js with the following exports: formatDate(isoString) → 'Apr 7, 2026', formatDateTime(isoString) → 'Apr 7, 2026 at 3:00 PM', formatRelative(isoString) → 'Today', 'Yesterday', '3 days ago', or falls back to formatDate for older dates
formatDate and formatDateTime always use { locale: 'en-US' } and the America/New_York fallback (or browser-detected locale via Intl.DateTimeFormat().resolvedOptions().timeZone)
Replace all inline date formatting in ApplicationList.jsx, DetailPanel.jsx, CalendarGrid.jsx, Analytics.jsx, and any other components — use the new utility functions instead
Remove any direct calls to .toLocaleDateString() or .toISOString().slice(0,10) in component files (those calls now live only in dateUtils.js)
Vitest tests for dateUtils.js: formatDate returns expected string, formatRelative returns 'Today' for today's date, 'Yesterday' for yesterday
npm test passes
userStories.notes: Post-v2 review: inconsistent date formatting across at least 4 components. CalendarGrid uses toISODate(), DetailPanel uses toLocaleDateString() without locale, ApplicationTimeline uses toLocaleDateString with full options.

userStories.title: Skeleton loaders for ApplicationList and Calendar month navigation
userStories.description: As a user, when my applications or calendar events are loading, I see either nothing or a centered 'Loading…' text. Skeleton loaders (animated placeholder rows/cells) are significantly better UX — they communicate structure while data loads and prevent layout shift.
acceptanceCriteria: Create frontend/src/components/SkeletonRow.jsx: a single skeleton application list row — gray animated shimmer bars matching the column layout (company, role, stage, date)
ApplicationList.jsx: when isLoading is true (first load, not refetching), render 8 SkeletonRow components instead of the empty state or spinner
ApplicationList.jsx: when isFetching is true but data already exists (background refresh), show a subtle top progress bar (thin animated stripe at top of list) instead of re-rendering skeletons
Create frontend/src/components/SkeletonCalendarCell.jsx: a skeleton day cell for the calendar grid
CalendarGrid.jsx: when calendar events are loading, render SkeletonCalendarCell in each day that has events (or all days if initial load)
StatsBar.jsx: when stats are loading, render 4 skeleton stat cards (gray rectangle placeholders) instead of actual values
Vitest test: ApplicationList renders 8 SkeletonRow when isLoading=true; renders real rows when data is present
npm test passes
userStories.notes: Loading UX is currently a centered 'Loading events…' text that blocks the full calendar grid. Skeleton loaders are the modern standard and dramatically improve perceived performance.

userStories.title: Mobile-responsive layout pass
userStories.description: As a user accessing Pipelined on a phone or tablet, the current layout breaks: the side-by-side ApplicationList + DetailPanel is too wide, the StatsBar overflows horizontally, and there is no bottom navigation. Add a mobile breakpoint layout that works on phones.
acceptanceCriteria: NavBar.jsx: on mobile (< md breakpoint), the desktop nav links are hidden and replaced with a hamburger icon; tapping it opens a slide-down menu with the same links

Dashboard.jsx: on mobile, DetailPanel is not shown inline — instead tapping an application row opens DetailPanel as a full-screen bottom sheet (fixed position, slides up from bottom, close button at top-right)
StatsBar.jsx: wraps to a 2×2 grid on mobile (sm: grid-cols-2) instead of the 4-column row that overflows
FilterBar.jsx: filter chips are horizontally scrollable on mobile (overflow-x-auto, whitespace-nowrap) instead of wrapping and pushing content down
CalendarGrid.jsx: on mobile, week row heights are reduced and event chips show only a colored dot (not the full company name)
All changes use Tailwind responsive prefixes (sm:, md:, lg:) — no media query in JS
Vitest tests mock window.innerWidth (or use matchMedia mock) to verify the mobile nav menu renders on small screens
npm test passes
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: Current layout is desktop-only. With StatsBar in a row of 4 and ApplicationList + DetailPanel side-by-side, the UI is unusable on a 390px phone screen.

userStories.title: Custom pipeline stages — user-level stage configuration
userStories.description: As a user, the default stages (Applied, Phone Screen, Onsite, Offer, Rejected) may not match my actual job search workflow. I want to rename, reorder, add, and remove stages that apply globally to all my applications — and have the stage selector everywhere reflect my custom list.
acceptanceCriteria: The users collection's default_stages field is already used — this story makes it editable

PATCH /api/auth/me accepts { default_stages: list[str] } and updates the user's default_stages; validates that the list has 2-10 items, each item is 1-40 chars, and 'Rejected' or an equivalent terminal stage is present (or allow any list — just validate count and length)
GET /api/auth/me returns default_stages in the UserResponse

New frontend page /settings (linked from NavBar user menu) with a 'Pipeline Stages' section: displays current stages as a sortable drag-and-drop list (use @dnd-kit/core already in or add to package.json), with add/remove/rename controls
Saving the list calls PATCH /api/auth/me and invalidates useAuth query cache
The stage selector dropdown in DetailPanel.jsx reads from the user's default_stages (via useAuth hook) instead of a hardcoded constant
ManualAddForm.jsx stage dropdown also uses default_stages
Bulk-stage-update dropdown also uses default_stages
New test in test_auth.py: PATCH /api/auth/me with valid default_stages returns 200; with >10 stages returns 422
Vitest test for Settings page: renders current stages, shows add input, calls mutation on save
All existing tests pass
Typecheck passes
npm test passes
userStories.notes: default_stages is already stored per-user but only set at registration. This makes it configurable via a settings page — a top power-user feature request.

userStories.title: Bulk CSV import for existing applications
userStories.description: As a user switching from a spreadsheet or another tracker, I want to import all my existing applications at once by uploading a CSV — instead of entering 80 applications one by one. This is critical for user onboarding and migration.
acceptanceCriteria: POST /api/applications/import accepts multipart/form-data with a file field (CSV, max 2MB)

Expected CSV columns (case-insensitive headers): role_title (required), company (required), stage (optional, defaults to 'Applied'), location (optional), remote_status (optional), compensation (optional), tags (optional, semicolon-separated), notes (optional), applied_at (optional, ISO date string)

The service parses the CSV using Python's csv module (no new dependencies), validates required fields, skips rows with missing required fields (records skipped count), deduplicates by (user_id, normalised_company, normalised_role)
Returns 200 { data: { imported: int, skipped: int, errors: [{row: int, reason: str}] } }
Import is capped at 500 rows per request; rows beyond 500 are ignored and a warning is included in the response

Frontend: Dashboard page adds an 'Import CSV' button next to the 'Export CSV' button; clicking opens a modal with a file input, a downloadable sample CSV template link, and an import progress summary after upload
After successful import, React Query cache for applications and stats is invalidated
test_applications.py adds tests: happy-path import with valid CSV, skip on missing required field, duplicate detection, 500-row cap
Vitest test for the import modal: file input renders, uploads file, shows result summary
All existing tests pass
Typecheck passes
npm test passes
userStories.notes: A major onboarding blocker. Users coming from Notion, Airtable, or Google Sheets can't get their data in without typing each entry. CSV import is table-stakes for a data tracking app.

userStories.title: Wellfound (AngelList) and Dice extension board support
userStories.description: As a user who browses startup jobs on Wellfound or tech jobs on Dice, I want the extension to detect these pages and offer the same one-click save experience.
acceptanceCriteria: extension/manifest.json adds content script matches for *://wellfound.com/jobs/* and *://www.dice.com/job-detail/*

extension/content/boards/wellfound.js exports an extract() function that reads: role_title from h1.job-title or [data-test='job-title'], company from .company-name or h2 near the job title, location from .location span, compensation from .compensation if present, company_type inferred from page context ('startup')

extension/content/boards/dice.js exports an extract() function that reads: role_title from h1.jobTitle, company from .employerInfo .name, location from .location-info, compensation from .salary-range if present
Both parsers follow the existing board parser interface: { role_title, company, location, remote_status, compensation, company_type, source }
content.js BOARD_PARSERS map includes 'wellfound.com' and 'dice.com' entries
Fallback to OpenAI page_text parsing applies when selectors return empty strings
extension/tests/test_wellfound.js and test_dice.js add JSDOM fixture tests (follow existing board test pattern)
All existing extension tests still pass
npm test passes in /extension
userStories.notes: Wellfound is the primary destination for startup jobs. Dice is heavily used for enterprise/government tech roles. Both are high-traffic sources for Pipelined's target users.

userStories.title: Extension popup redesign with richer save history and one-click re-save
userStories.description: As a user, the current extension popup shows a minimal list of recent saves. I want a richer popup with company info, application stage, a status badge for each save, and a quick-action button to open the full application in the dashboard.
acceptanceCriteria: extension/popup/popup.html and popup.js are redesigned: each recent save card shows company name (bold), role title, stage badge (colored pill matching stage color), and relative time ('2 days ago')
Each card has an 'Open in dashboard' link that opens {PIPELINED_URL}/dashboard?highlight={app_id} in a new tab
If the user is not logged in (no token in chrome.storage.session), the popup shows a 'Sign in to Pipelined' button linking to the login page
Popup renders at 320px × 480px (fixed, consistent with popup.html width)
The popup header shows the user's display_name and a sign-out button (which clears chrome.storage.session)

Stage badge colors match the frontend constants: Applied=blue, Phone Screen=purple, Onsite=orange, Offer=green, Rejected=red (use the same hex values from shared/constants if they exist, or inline them)
extension/tests/test_popup.js adds tests: renders save cards with stage badge, renders sign-in state when no token, open-in-dashboard link has correct href
All existing extension tests still pass
npm test passes in /extension
userStories.notes: Current popup is minimal — just a list of save titles. The redesign makes the extension feel like a polished product, not a utility. Companies and stage badges are stored in chrome.storage.local already.

userStories.title: CSRF protection for all cookie-authenticated mutations
userStories.description: As an operator, all POST/PATCH/DELETE endpoints that use httpOnly JWT cookies for auth are vulnerable to CSRF. Since modern browsers have SameSite=Lax defaults this is mitigated in most scenarios, but explicit CSRF protection is needed for a production-grade app.
acceptanceCriteria: backend/middleware/csrf.py implements a double-submit cookie pattern: on every GET /api/auth/me response (or a new GET /api/csrf endpoint), set a non-httpOnly CSRF cookie (name: 'pipelined_csrf', Secure, SameSite=Strict, random 32-byte hex value)

A CSRF middleware class (FastAPI dependency or Starlette middleware) validates that the X-CSRF-Token request header matches the csrf cookie value for all POST, PATCH, DELETE requests (skip validation for GET, HEAD, OPTIONS)
Endpoints excluded from CSRF: GET /health, POST /api/auth/login, POST /api/auth/register, POST /api/auth/google (these use JSON body, not cookies, or are pre-auth)
On mismatch, return 403 with error code CSRF_TOKEN_MISMATCH
frontend/src/api/ axios instance is updated to automatically read the pipelined_csrf cookie and include it as X-CSRF-Token header on all requests
frontend/src/hooks/useAuth.js: on app load (useEffect), call GET /api/auth/me to ensure the CSRF cookie is set before any mutations are attempted
test_health.py or new test_csrf.py adds tests: POST without CSRF token returns 403, POST with matching token succeeds
All existing tests pass (update test client to include CSRF token in request headers)
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Post-v2 security review finding. Cookie-based auth without CSRF protection is a standard OWASP vulnerability. Double-submit cookie is the right pattern for a SPA.

userStories.title: Timezone-aware calendar events with user timezone preference
userStories.description: As a user in a non-UTC timezone, interview times stored as UTC datetimes appear wrong in the calendar (e.g., a 10am PST interview shows as 6pm on the calendar). The system needs to store and display times in the user's local timezone.
acceptanceCriteria: users collection gets a new field: timezone: str = 'America/New_York' (IANA timezone string, default Eastern for US-centric users)
PATCH /api/auth/me accepts { timezone: str } and validates it is a valid IANA timezone string (use zoneinfo.available_timezones() for validation)
GET /api/auth/me returns timezone in UserResponse

Settings page (from US-068) adds a 'Timezone' selector: a searchable <select> listing common IANA timezones grouped by region, defaulting to the user's current browser timezone (Intl.DateTimeFormat().resolvedOptions().timeZone)

frontend/src/lib/dateUtils.js formatDateTime() reads the user's timezone from AuthContext and uses Intl.DateTimeFormat with timeZone option to display all calendar event times in the user's local timezone
CalendarGrid.jsx event chips show times in the user's local timezone
CalendarEventDetail.jsx shows event start/end in user's local timezone with a note '(Your local time)'
test_auth.py adds a test: PATCH /api/auth/me with invalid timezone string returns 422
Vitest test for dateUtils.js: formatDateTime with a known UTC string and America/Los_Angeles timezone returns the correct Pacific time
All existing tests pass
Typecheck passes
npm test passes
userStories.notes: Critical usability bug for users outside the server's timezone. All calendar events are stored as UTC but displayed naively, causing interview times to appear wrong.

userStories.title: Weekly email digest with job search summary and stale application nudges
userStories.description: As a user, I want to receive a weekly email every Monday morning summarizing my job search progress: how many applications I submitted this week, which applications are stale, and any interviews scheduled for the coming week. This keeps the job search active and Pipelined top-of-mind.
acceptanceCriteria: backend/notifications/ directory is created with email_service.py (SMTP via smtplib or SendGrid SDK if SENDGRID_API_KEY is set, otherwise logs to structlog in dev/test mode)
config.py adds: smtp_host, smtp_port, smtp_from_email, smtp_password (all optional), sendgrid_api_key (optional), digest_enabled: bool = True
backend/notifications/digest.py implements build_weekly_digest(user_id) → Digested data model and send_weekly_digest(user_id) → bool

The digest includes: total applications this week (last 7 days), total active pipeline count, list of stale applications (name, company, days since update) for apps untouched > 14 days, upcoming calendar events in next 7 days (role, company, date/time)
APScheduler job in main.py's lifespan: runs every Monday at 8am UTC, calls send_weekly_digest for all users who have digest_enabled=True (stored as user preference field)
PATCH /api/auth/me accepts { digest_enabled: bool } to opt in/out
Settings page adds a 'Weekly digest email' toggle switch (on by default for new users)
Email is plain text with a clear call-to-action link back to the dashboard (no HTML email in v1 of this feature)
test_digest.py (new file) adds: build_weekly_digest returns correct stale app count, upcoming events list; send_weekly_digest calls email_service with correct content (mock email_service)
All existing tests pass
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Retention feature. A weekly digest email is one of the highest-ROI features for a job tracker — it brings users back to the app on a regular cadence without requiring them to remember to check.

userStories.title: Salary insights: distribution chart in Analytics and range filter on job board
userStories.description: As a user, compensation data is stored on every application and job listing but never analyzed. I want to see a salary distribution histogram in Analytics (to understand market rates I'm targeting) and a salary range slider filter on the job board (to find listings above my minimum).
acceptanceCriteria: backend/applications/service.py get_analytics() adds a salary_distribution facet: groups applications with non-empty compensation by parsed numeric value buckets ($0-50k, $50-100k, $100-150k, $150-200k, $200k+) — parse compensation strings to extract numeric values (handle '120000', '$120k', '$120,000', '120k-150k' formats using regex)
GET /api/applications/analytics response adds salary_distribution: [{ bucket: str, count: int }]
Analytics.jsx adds a Compensation Distribution BarChart (recharts) below the existing charts showing the bucket counts
GET /api/jobs accepts min_salary: int | None and max_salary: int | None query params
jobs/service.py applies salary filter: when min_salary is set, filter listings where the parsed numeric salary >= min_salary
Frontend JobBoard adds a salary range input (two number inputs: Min $ and Max $) that passes min_salary and max_salary to the query
test_jobs.py adds tests for salary filter endpoint
test_applications.py adds a test for salary_distribution in the analytics aggregation
All existing tests pass
Typecheck passes
npm test passes
userStories.notes: Salary data is stored on every application and listing but never surfaced in any analytics. This is a high-value insight for users actively negotiating offers or setting target ranges.

userStories.title: Undo delete and undo archive with 5-second toast
userStories.description: As a user, accidentally deleting or archiving an application is irreversible and devastating if it had notes, timeline history, or linked interviews. A 5-second undo toast gives a safety net without requiring a full soft-delete infrastructure change.
acceptanceCriteria: In frontend/src/components/UndoToast.jsx, create a toast component that: appears at the bottom-center of the screen, shows 'Application deleted' or 'Application archived' with an 'Undo' button, auto-dismisses after 5 seconds with a visual countdown progress bar
The Undo button is only available for 5 seconds — clicking it calls the undo action and dismisses the toast

For delete: the undo action calls POST /api/applications/{id}/restore (new endpoint) which undeletes the document — implement a soft-delete approach: DELETE /api/applications/{id} now sets deleted: true and deleted_at: datetime rather than removing the document; a background task hard-deletes documents where deleted=True and deleted_at < now - 1 day
ApplicationDocument schema adds: deleted: bool = False, deleted_at: datetime | None = None
GET /api/applications default filter excludes deleted=True documents
POST /api/applications/{id}/restore sets deleted=False, deleted_at=None; returns 200
For archive: the undo action calls PATCH /api/applications/{id}/unarchive (already exists from US-048)

ApplicationList.jsx and DetailPanel.jsx: after calling the delete or archive mutation, show the UndoToast instead of immediately invalidating the cache; only invalidate the cache when the toast auto-dismisses (no undo clicked) OR when navigating away
test_applications.py adds tests for: DELETE sets deleted=True not hard deletes, POST /restore resets deleted fields, deleted applications excluded from default list
Vitest test for UndoToast: renders with countdown, undo button calls callback, auto-dismisses after timeout
All existing tests pass
Typecheck passes
npm test passes
userStories.notes: Users have reported losing applications with notes and history due to accidental deletes. The 5s undo pattern (used by Gmail, Notion) is the gold standard UX for destructive actions.

userStories.title: Application sharing — generate a read-only public link to your pipeline
userStories.description: As a user working with a career counselor, mentor, or accountability partner, I want to generate a shareable read-only link to my job application pipeline so they can view my progress without needing a Pipelined account. This is a differentiated social feature no competing tracker has.
acceptanceCriteria: POST /api/sharing/create creates a share token: a random 16-character alphanumeric slug stored in a shares collection with { user_id, slug, created_at, expires_at (30 days from now), is_active: true }
GET /api/sharing/my returns the user's active share token (or null)
DELETE /api/sharing/revoke deactivates the current token (is_active=False)

GET /api/public/{slug} is a new PUBLIC (no auth) endpoint that returns a read-only snapshot of the user's applications: { data: { display_name: str, stats: StatsResponse, applications: [{ role_title, company, stage, applied_at, stage_history }] } } — excludes: notes, compensation, tags, personal data
GET /api/public/{slug} returns 404 if slug is invalid or is_active=False or expires_at < now
New frontend page /public/{slug} that renders a read-only pipeline view: same StatsBar, same application list (without notes/compensation columns), with a 'Join Pipelined →' CTA in the header
Settings page adds a 'Share my pipeline' section with: a toggle to generate/revoke the link, a copy-to-clipboard button showing the full shareable URL, expiry countdown
New index on shares collection: { slug: 1 } unique, { expires_at: 1 } TTL for auto-cleanup
test_sharing.py (new file) adds: POST creates share, GET /public/{slug} returns correct public data, revoked slug returns 404, expired slug returns 404
Vitest test for the public pipeline page: renders StatsBar and application list from mock data
All existing tests pass
Typecheck passes
npm test passes
userStories.notes: Zero competing job trackers have public sharing. This is a viral growth vector — shared pipelines drive new user signups from the CTA. Mentors and career counselors sharing Pipelined with their students creates organic growth.
